volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Captives (Section 5.3.990 - Section 5.4.1035)

sections.label: Section 5.3.990
sections.original: Quid tu ais ?
sections.translation: What do you say ?

sections.label: Section 5.3.990b
sections.original: Quin istic ipsust Tyndarus tuos filius ,
sections.translation: Why , this very Tyndarus is your son , according , indeed , to the proofs that he mentions . For , a boy himself together with me from boyhood was he brought up , virtuously and modestly , even to manhood .

sections.label: Section 5.3.993
sections.original: Et miser sum et fortunatus , si vos vera dicitis ;
sections.translation: I am both unhappy and happy , if you are telling the truth . Unhappy for this reason , because , if he is my son , I have badly treated him .

sections.label: Section 5.3.995
sections.original: eheu , quóm ego plus minusve feci quam me aequom fuit .
sections.translation: Alas ! why have I done both more and less than was his due . That I have ill treated him I am grieved ; would that it only could be undone . But see , he ’ s coming here , in a guise not according to his deserts .

sections.label: Section 5.4.998
sections.original: Vidi ego multa saepe picta , quae Acherunti fierent
sections.translation: I have seen many of the torments which take place at Acheron [*] often represented in paintings [*] ; but most certainly there is no Acheron equal

sections.label: Section 5.4.1000
sections.original: atque ubi ego fui , in lapicidinis . illic ibi demumst locus ,
sections.translation: to where I have been in the stone-quarries . There , in fine , is the place where real lassitude must be undergone by the body in laboriousness . For when I came there , just as either jackdaws , or ducks , or quails , are given to Patrician children [*] , for them to play with , so in like fashion , when I arrived , a crow was given [*] me with which to amuse myself .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1005
sections.original: sed erus eccum ante ostium , et erus alter eccum ex Alide
sections.translation: But see , my master ’ s before the door ; and lo ! my other master has returned from Elis .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1006b
sections.original: Salve , éxoptate gnate mi .
sections.translation: Hail to you , my much wished-for son .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1006c
sections.original: Hem , quid gnate mi ?
sections.translation: Ha ! how — my son ? Aye , aye , I know why you pretend yourself to be the father , and me to be the son ; it is because , just as parents do , you give me the means of seeing the light [*] .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1009
sections.original: Salve , Tyndare .
sections.translation: Hail to you , Tyndarus .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1009b
sections.original: Et tu , quoius causa hanc aerumnam exigo .
sections.translation: And to you , for whose sake I am enduring these miseries .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1010
sections.original: At nunc liber in divitias faxo venies . nam tibi
sections.translation: But now I ’ ll make you in freedom come to wealth . For ( pointing to HEGIO ) this is your father ; ( pointing to STALAGMUS ) that is the slave who stole you away from here when four years old , and sold you to my father for six minae . He gave you , when a little child , to me a little child for my own service . He ( pointing to STALAGMUS ) has made a confession , for we have brought him back from Elis .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1015
sections.original: Quid huius filium ?
sections.translation: How , where ’ s Hegio ’ s son ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.1015b
sections.original: Intus eccum fratrem germanum tuom .
sections.translation: Look now ; indoors is your own brother .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1017
sections.original: Quin , inquam , intus hic est .
sections.translation: Why , he ’ s in-doors , I say .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1018
sections.original: Nunc tibi páter hic est : hic fur est tuos , qui parvom hinc te abstulit .
sections.translation: Now this is your own father ; ( pointing to STALAGMUS ) this is the thief who stole you when a little child .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1019
sections.original: At ego hunc grandis grandem natu ob furtum ad carnificem dabo .
sections.translation: But now , grown up , I shall give him grown up to the executioner for his thieving .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1020
sections.original: Meritus est .
sections.translation: He deserves it .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1020b
sections.original: Ergo edepol merito meritam mercedem dabo .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ ll deservedly give him the reward that he deserves . ( To HEGIO .) But tell me I pray you , are you my father ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.1021b
sections.original: Ego sum , gnate mi .
sections.translation: I am he , my son .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1022
sections.original: Nunc demum in memoriam redeo , cum mecum recogito .
sections.translation: Now , at length , I bring it to my recollection , when I reconsider with myself : troth , I do now at last recall to memory that I had heard , as though through a mist , that my father was called Hegio .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1025
sections.original: Is ego sum .
sections.translation: I am he .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1025b
sections.original: Compedibus quaeso ut tibi sit levior filius
sections.translation: I pray that your son may be lightened of these fetters , and this slave be loaded with them .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1026b
sections.original: Certum est principio id praevortier .
sections.translation: I ’ m resolved that that shall be the first thing attended to . Let ’ s go in-doors , that the blacksmith may be sent for , in order that I may remove those fetters from you , and give them to him . ( They go into the house .)

sections.label: Section 5.4.1028b
sections.original: Quoi peculi nihil est , recte feceris .
sections.translation: To one who has no savings of his own , you ’ ll be rightly doing so [*] .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1029
sections.original: Spectatores , ad pudicos mores facta haec fabula est ,
sections.translation: Spectators , this play is founded on chaste manners .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1030
sections.original: neque in hac subigitationes sunt neque ulla amatio
sections.translation: No wenching is there in this , and no intriguing , no exposure of a child , no cheating out of money ; and no young man in love here make his mistress free without his father ’ s knowledge . The Poets find but few Comedies [*] of this kind , where good men might become better . Now , if it pleases you ,

sections.label: Section 5.4.1035
sections.original: et si placuimus neque odio fuimus , signum hoc mittite :
sections.translation: and if we have pleased you , and have not been tedious , do you give this sign of it : you who wish that chaste manners should have their reward , give us your applause .
